name: Eastern Roman (Byzantine) imperial continuity: Imperium Romanum → Βασιλεία Ῥωμαίων (27 BC–1453)
description: Succession-continuity chain of the Roman Empire as a single juridical and cultural entity through its transition from a pan-Mediterranean polity with co-imperial administration (after the 395 Theodosian partition) to the Eastern-only Roman Empire headquartered at Constantinople after the 476 extinction of the Western imperial line. The state called itself βασιλεία Τῶν Ῥωμαίων ('Empire of the Romans') and its inhabitants Ῥωμαῖοι ('Romans') throughout its entire existence — the term 'Byzantine' is a modern construct (first used in the 16th century by Hieronymus Wolf, popularised in the 19th century) to distinguish the post-Constantinian Greek-speaking empire from the Latin-speaking Roman Republic and early principate. The chain ends with the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople on 29 May 1453 under Mehmed II 'Fatih' (the Conqueror). Parallel micro-successor states of 1204–1461 (the Empire of Nicaea, Empire of Trebizond, Despotate of Epirus) are documented as entity records but not on this trunk; the 1204–1261 Latin Empire of Constantinople (Imperium Romaniae) is similarly documented as entity but not on this trunk because it represents a Crusader-Frankish rupture rather than a Roman-imperial continuity. The Palaiologan restoration (1261) under Michael VIII effectively continues the Βασιλεία Ῥωμαίων entity rather than instituting a new polity.
ethical_notes: Four points of care. (a) The chain terminates juridically at 1453 but the Roman-imperial imaginary persisted for centuries afterward: the Ottoman sultans styled themselves 'Kayser-i Rûm' (Caesar of Rome) after the conquest — Mehmed II explicitly claimed the Roman imperial title, purchased relics of the apostle Andrew, and considered a Rome-campaign in the 1460s — and in Russian doctrine after 1472 (when Ivan III married Sophia Palaiologina, niece of the last emperor Constantine XI) Moscow articulated the 'Third Rome' doctrine (formalised by the monk Philotheus of Pskov ~1510: 'two Romes have fallen, the third stands, and a fourth there will not be'). Both appropriations are ideological and not on this chain. (b) The 1204 Fourth Crusade — during which Latin-Christian crusaders diverted by Venice from their intended Jerusalem campaign besieged and sacked Constantinople for three days (12–15 April 1204), killing tens of thousands of fellow Christians, raping nuns and civilians, looting the Hagia Sophia (a prostitute was seated on the Patriarchal throne and made to sing bawdy songs, per Niketas Choniates), dismembering the bronze horses of the hippodrome (now on St Mark's Basilica, Venice), and establishing the Latin Empire of Constantinople — produced a 57-year interruption that is the single most severe rupture in Roman imperial continuity. Pope John Paul II formally apologised to the Patriarch of Constantinople in 2001 for this event. The Orthodox memory of 1204 is one of the foundational wounds of East-West Christian division and conditions all subsequent Ottoman-era Orthodox politics. (c) The 1453 conquest by Mehmed II included a three-day sack under the customary Islamic laws of war for a city taken by storm (rather than peaceful surrender), producing large-scale civilian casualties, mass enslavement, and the conversion of the Hagia Sophia into a mosque (Fatih Sultan Mehmet's proclamation on the afternoon of 29 May 1453); the last emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos died fighting at the walls near the Gate of St Romanos, his body never definitively identified. Mehmed II then instituted the millet system, preserving Orthodox patriarchal jurisdiction over Orthodox Christians and explicitly installing Gennadios Scholarios as ecumenical patriarch in January 1454 — a deliberate Ottoman policy of continuity-through-incorporation. (d) The chain does not represent the parallel Armenian (Bagratid, Cilician), Georgian (Bagrationi), Bulgarian (First and Second Empires), and Serbian (Nemanjić, Dušan's Empire) imperial claims, each of which at various points declared imperial titles in deliberate reference to or rivalry with Constantinople. Dusan of Serbia was crowned 'Цар Ρωμαίων καὶ Σέρβων' (Emperor of the Romans and Serbs) at Skopje in 1346; Simeon I of Bulgaria ('Τζαὶς τῶν Βουλγάρων καὶ Ῥωμαίων') declared himself emperor in 913 to Constantinople's consternation. These are entity-level records but not on this trunk.

sources.citation: Procopius of Caesarea (d. c. 565). De Bellis, De Aedificiis, Anekdota — primary for Justinianic era.
Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos (d. 959). De Administrando Imperio — primary for 10th-century diplomacy and legitimising ideology.
Anna Komnene (d. 1153). Ἀλεξιάς (Alexiad) — primary for Komnenian era and the First Crusade.
Niketas Choniates (d. 1217). Χρονικὴ διήγησις — primary for the 1204 Sack of Constantinople.
Georgios Sphrantzes (d. c. 1478). Χρονικόν — primary for the 1453 Fall of Constantinople.
Laonikos Chalkokondyles (d. c. 1490). Ἀπόδειξις Ὠστοριῶν — primary for late Palaiologan era.
Ostrogorsky, G. (1940, 3rd ed. 1968). Geschichte des byzantinischen Staates / History of the Byzantine State. Rutgers UP — canonical 20th-century synthesis.
Treadgold, W. (1997). A History of the Byzantine State and Society. Stanford UP.
Kazhdan, A. (ed.) (1991). The Oxford Dictionary of Byzantium, 3 vols. Oxford UP — reference standard.
Runciman, S. (1965). The Fall of Constantinople 1453. Cambridge UP.
Philips, J. (2004). The Fourth Crusade and the Sack of Constantinople. Pimlico.
Kaldellis, A. (2019). Romanland: Ethnicity and Empire in Byzantium. Harvard UP — central for the 'they called themselves Romans' argument.
Angold, M. (2003). The Fourth Crusade: Event and Context. Pearson.

links.description: The Roman Empire from Augustus (27 BC) through the Theodosian partition of January 395, when on Theodosius I's death the empire was divided between his elder son Arcadius (East, Constantinople) and his younger son Honorius (West, Milan/Ravenna). The division was administrative rather than juridical — Roman imperial ideology held that there was a single empire with two augusti — but from 395 the two halves developed increasingly distinct trajectories.
links.ethical_notes: See the Imperium Romanum ethical note on the Western continuity chain (batch_07) for a full treatment of the costs of Roman imperial foundation and expansion. For the specific context of this chain: the 4th-century Roman state was by the Theodosian period a Christianising empire (the Edict of Thessalonica, 380, made Nicene Christianity the state religion), producing internal religious violence against polytheists (temple destructions, persecution of paganism through the Theodosian and Justinianic codes), Jews (legal disabilities progressively codified), and heterodox Christians (Arians, Donatists, Monophysites, Nestorians). The transition to an explicitly Christian Roman Empire is foundational to the political theology of the entire Byzantine continuity tracked on this chain.

links.description: The Roman Empire of the East, headquartered at Constantinople (founded by Constantine I in 330 as Nova Roma on the site of the Greek colony of Byzantion), continuing as the sole surviving Roman imperial polity after the 476 extinction of the Western imperial line. The state maintained continuous Roman legal tradition (the Codex Theodosianus 438; Justinian's Corpus Iuris Civilis 528–534 with its Digest, Institutes, Code, and Novels, the foundational text of Western civil law); continuous imperial succession through ~93 emperors over 1058 years (395–1453); and continuous Orthodox Christian ecclesiastical organisation centred on the Patriarchate of Constantinople (the Great Schism with Rome formalised in 1054). Key periods: the Justinianic reconquest (530s–560s), the Heraclian wars with Sasanian Persia (602–628), the 7th-century Arab conquests that stripped Syria, Egypt, and North Africa, the Macedonian dynasty recovery (867–1056), the Komnenian restoration (1081–1185), the 1204–1261 Latin occupation (entity: Imperium Romaniae), the Palaiologan restoration (1261–1453), and the final decline and 1453 conquest. Capital: Constantinople. Official Christian confession: Chalcedonian Orthodoxy (from 451; the Great Schism with Rome 1054).
links.ethical_notes: The 1058-year Byzantine continuity contains major episodes of internal and external violence: the Nika riots (532) where Justinian's massacre of ~30,000 Blues-and-Greens rioters in the Hippodrome consolidated his rule; the Heraclian-Sasanian war (602–628) with extensive Anatolian and Mesopotamian devastation; the iconoclast controversy (726–843) with forced destruction of icons and persecution of iconodules including mass arrests of monks; the Macedonian-era Bulgarian wars, climaxing in Basil II's blinding of 14,000 captured Bulgarian soldiers at Kleidion (1014, earning him the epithet 'Βουλγαροκτόνος' — Bulgar-Slayer); the Komnenian massacre of Latin residents in Constantinople (April 1182, ~60,000 killed and deported by the populace with imperial complicity, which directly motivated Latin revanche in 1204); the systematic Byzantine persecution of the Paulicians (7th–9th century, forced relocations from Anatolia to the Balkans) and Bogomils (10th–12th century). The 1204 Latin sack of Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade — ~3 days of rape, murder, and plunder by ostensibly Christian Crusader forces diverted from their Jerusalem mission by Venetian fleet pricing pressure — killed an uncounted number (Niketas Choniates' eyewitness account is the canonical source), destroyed the Great Library of Constantinople (the last major classical library in the Christian world), and looted the bronze horses now on St Mark's Basilica in Venice. The subsequent 57-year Latin occupation (1204–1261) profoundly weakened the empire; the Palaiologan recovery under Michael VIII was never financially or territorially complete, and the state from ~1320 onward lost steadily to the Ottoman beyşs in Anatolia and the Serbian empire in the Balkans. The final Ottoman siege of Constantinople (6 April – 29 May 1453) ended with Constantine XI Palaiologos's death in combat at the walls; Mehmed II allowed three days of sack (reduced to one by some accounts, as the city was already largely depopulated from the Black Death and earlier catastrophes) and converted Hagia Sophia into a mosque on the afternoon of 29 May. The Ottoman millet system preserved Orthodox ecclesiastical jurisdiction, and Mehmed II installed Gennadios Scholarios as ecumenical patriarch in January 1454 as a deliberate policy of continuity-through-incorporation. The Byzantine-Ottoman transition is thus a conquest that nevertheless preserved institutional structures of Orthodox Christian life under new Muslim political sovereignty.
